video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Passengers; Prams; 1900-1909; Sea ferries; Steam launches; Seacombe | locais: Seacombe | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:cyMGx5cTqxXa4q2euUfAELT564x6M7va | M&K 210: Note: Most of the men are wearing coats and bowler hats and it is probable that they are going to or from work. M&K 210,211 and 212 make up this one film title. | Ferries across the Mersey show Edwardian workers on their daily commute to and from Liverpool. | Three views of Mersey ferries taking workers to and from Edwardian Liverpool are collected here: the Snowdrop arrives at Seacombe, workers flood out of one of the ferry ports and the 'Lily' heads back to Liverpool. The comings and goings of ferries between Liverpool and the Wirral are a distinctive feature of the city's life, and the passengers seen here highlight the diversity of its workforce.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Liverpool; Royal Navy; Asian peoples; Black soldiers; Trafalgar Day | locais: Liverpool | som: Silent | duração: 3 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:x0ZHJvcDpnUqjnTEx7LEDChUC2yQ6xaE | Scenes at Trafalgar Day (21 October) celebrations in Castle Street, Liverpool at the base of Nelson's Monument. | Huge crowds turn out in Edwardian Liverpool for the annual celebrations marking the victory at the Battle of Trafalgar. | Nearly a century after Nelson's famous victory, the British were still celebrating his achievements and by extension the British Navy, which had secured the Empire ever since. Here, a dizzying array of naval officers, cadets, Lascars and crews from the Liverpool-based merchant marine (the Allan Line, Cunard and the Elder Dempster Co.) march past after a stirring speech from the Lord Mayor. | The grand procession, organised by the Liverpool branch of the Navy League, is testimony to an era when Britain's navy was the undisputed ruler of the seas. Some black sailors are visible among the marchers; they are among a handful of black faces appearing across the Mitchell & Kenyon collection of films, at a time long before mass immigration. As everyone gathers in Castle Street for Trafalgar Day at the base of Nelson's Column, with its endless bunting and uni...

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: Rugby matches; Dewsbury (rugby league team); Manningham (rugby league team) | locais: Dewsbury | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:xyZDNrcDpceFSAXq2N4Xtyp4C9aXjahc | A record of a Rugby League match between Dewsbury and Manningham at Dewsbury's Crown Flatt ground. | Rare film of Manningham FC's days as a rugby club, in a hard-fought contest at Dewsbury's Crown Flatt ground. | You might think an Edwardian rugby match would be a relatively gentlemanly affair, but not on the evidence of these torn shirts and bruised faces. After the players ceremoniously trot on to the pitch, the film is almost pure sporting action - unusually for Mitchell and Kenyon's productions, which typically presented a healthy portion of crowd shots to drum up interest in later film shows. | Manningham won the inaugural Northern Rugby Football Union season back in 1896, but the club's life in competitive rugby was to be shortlived. The Bradford side would play just eight seasons of the infant rugby league game before defecting to the round ball and re-inventing themselves as Bradford City Association Football Club in 1903. This film appears to be the only surviving footage of the club's rugby past.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: Liverpool Football Club | locais: Liverpool | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:s0dTFtcDr85bZwgJXBp-mEuCsnEFR9o8 | Early league football, with the team who will become Birmingham City losing at Anfield in a relegation year. | Footage of this First Division football game serves as a brief appetiser for shots of enthusiastically waving crowds and an unusually prolonged group portrait shot of a handsome-looking fire brigade. The reason for their feature role is lost, but they beautifully illustrate the power of moving image to bring history to life when they drop their static poses and relax in front of the camera. | The game ended with a 3-1home win to Liverpool, the previous season's champions, over newly-promoted Small Heath. This game came less than a month after the two teams saw out a 0-0 draw in Birmingham on the opening day of the season. Liverpool fielded seven Scottish players, at a time when Scotland dominated the British Home Championship, revealing that the club's strong links north of the border longprecede Shankly, Dalglish, Souness and Hansen. Small Heath, who changed their name to Birmingham in 1905 before settling on Birmingham City in 1943, finished the season 17th out of 18th in the table, and returned to the Seco...

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: Cricket; Cricket matches; Lancashire (cricket team); Old Trafford (cricket ground); Bowling (cricket); Somerset (cricket team); Scandals | locais: Old Trafford Cricket Ground | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:xlaHZscDpws_f3BKS7_ve05CmZnqhoG4 | Scenes at a cricket match between Lancashire and Somerset at Old Trafford, where Arthur Mold's bowling action (and credibility as a sportsman) becomes the centre of a sporting controversy. | Did he bowl or did he throw? A fascinating record of an early cricketing controversy. | This unusually topical Mitchell and Kenyon title is perhaps the first filmed record of a sporting controversy. Lancashire's Mold, one of cricket's leading fast bowlers, was judged to have broken rules with his unorthodox bowling action. Despite his protests, this footage proves him guilty of 'throwing'. Mold's teammate Arthur Hornby was another cricketing giant, captaining Lancashire and England. | The film was taken during the second day of play between Lancashire and Somerset at Old Trafford. On the first, Mold, had been no-balled 15 times in 10 overs (including five times in one over) by Australian umpire James Phillips for contravening Rule 48. The accusation caused much comment in the local press. The Northampt...

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: Rugby matches | locais: Halifax | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:o3bzZtcDrTaA2O58U7iw-as6gNJULxFt | Note: The title is given as HULL FOOTBALL NO. 2 but they are playing rugby league . The players are likely to be Hull Kingston Rovers. | A rugby league game shows the sport in transition, on a bitterly cold day. | Though the camera can't always follow the pace, this record of a Yorkshire v Lancashire contest is one of the best of Mitchell and Kenyon's rugby films. Shot on a bitter February day, it boasts sharp, lively images of play and camera positions that bring us almost nose-to-nose with the players. Spectators are packed tightly on the terraces, which might at least have helped keep the cold at bay.

video_title: Employees Leaving North Eastern Engine Works, Gateshead (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Rail workshops; Gateshead | locais: Gateshead | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:BpMjdtcDr7hfYmYHSe2o_Ki_ilZmmbQk | Engineering factory gate. | A tide of flat-capped rail factory workers is ushered past the camera after a day's work. | Mitchell and Kenyon's numerous ‘factory gate' films might look like social documentaries today, but they were originally commissioned as money-spinners for local film shows. Crowds of workers leaving for home would be herded and cajoled past the camera, then, a day or two later, encouraged by flyers and posters to buy tickets for a screening of the film and the thrill of recognising themselves.
video_title_display: Employees Leaving North Eastern Engine Works, Gateshead (1901)

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: West Yorkshire; Rugby matches; 1900-1909; Spectators; Rugby league; Leeds (Rugby league team); Hunslet (Rugby league team); Hunslet | locais: Hunslet | som: Silent | duração: 4 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:00bjZtcDqIk-zLp_321ImkgZs2PE87nL | A rugby league match. | A less-than-serious Leeds team take a four-goals-and-two-tries battering from Hunslet. | Although it's a far cry from the elaborate sports coverage we know today, this is a sophisticated and evocative record of an Edwardian Northern Union rugby derby. It's striking how quickly play resumes after the breakdown, with scrums forming almost immediately. Watch out for Hunslet's (white jerseys) star player "Arh" Albert Goldthorpe, experimentally picked at half back. | Albert Goldthorpe, one of five brothers to play for Hunslet (three of them may be in this film), would later earn fame and glory as captain of the Hunslet Rugby League side of 1907-08 - the first ever to win the four trophies, or 'All Four Cups', in the same season. Goldthorpe retired from playing in 1910, and stood down from Hunslet's administrative committees in 1931, spending the rest of his working life running a milk round in Headingley.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: Rugby matches; Spectators; St. Helens (Rugby league team); Swinton (Rugby league team) | locais: St Helens | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:p3cDBtcDrJ9SCHvZbGOrjaqQ2_S7a_MX | Rugby. | The crowds at Knowsley Road await a Rugby league match between the Saints and the Lions. | If there ever was any footage of actual play in St Helens 6-3 victory over the previous season's Challenge Cup winners, it's now sadly lost. But at least these images of the happy home crowd survive. The seemingly ubiquitous twirling hat gesture presumably fell out of fashion as hats became less common - although a lack of headgear doesn't stop the boy in front of the fence from joining in.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: Rugby matches; Oldham (Rugby league team); St. Helens (Rugby league team) | locais: Oldham | som: Silent | duração: 3 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:A1bjFtcDrRY0Gzg8hK0wUcX_iKCa8-m4 | A rugby match. | The evolution of Rugby League in a top of the table match between rival Lancashire clubs. | A crowd of 20,000 watched this top-of-the-table Lancashire League clash at Oldham's Watersheddings ground. After the breakaway from the Rugby Football Union in 1895, the Northern Union gradually introduced rule changes. By 1906 the 15-a-side game, as here, would become 13-a-side, while 'play the ball' replaced the use of scrums to restart after every tackle. Oldham emerged 1901's League winners. | The Northern Rugby Football Union, better known as Northern Union, was formed in 1895, when prominent Yorkshire and Lancashire clubs resigned from the Rugby Football Union in a dispute over compensation for players taking time off work. All the major differences between the two codes would be established by 1907.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: Athletics; Policemen; Long jump; Sports; 1900-1909; Sporting awards; Steeplechasing (running); Pole vaulting; Huddersfield; Sporting competitions; Shot putting | locais: Fartown Ground, Huddersfield | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:k2eHZscDqtc4S6u4yeSyT-HI3dBZIY_- | M&K 592: Various athletics in close-up, starting with two males poll vaulting and followed by a man throwing the hammer. | The annual championship meeting of England's premier athletics association. | The annual Amateur Athletic Association (AAA) championship was Edwardian England's premier athletics meeting. The 1901 Championships were held at Huddersfield Cricket & Athletic Club on Saturday 6 July. Ten athletes are seen: four American, two Irish, and four English, and this international aspect is reflected in vignettes of the champions posing for the camera. | The film includes shots of some of the winners of the individual events, including the USA's Arthur Duffey, the world's premier sprinter and winner of the 100 yard sprint. Although the American athletes appeared as amateurs, their level of support and sophisticated training regimes gave them a marked advantage over the less well-resourced British athletes.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Funfairs; Horse-drawn transport; Carlisle; Algie's circus | locais: Carlisle | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:c5c3ZscDqBgq0lCaWkSHpkdp4kVhLBLG | Algie's Circus proceeding through Carlisle. | The circus comes to town in wintery Carlisle. | These scenes of the advance guard of a modest circus riding through Carlisle's streets, accompanied by a growing crowd of citizens and children, give us a good idea of the scale of the regional touring circus show. The party includes carriages, two white horses (one carrying a dog), clowns and a band, preceded by a man with a placard advertising entertainments, including a 'Bioscope' (film) show.

This Great Western Railway footage, initially screened in Plymouth, is one of their southernmost surviving works. Taking in the Royal Albert Bridge and several other interesting geographical details, it also catches passing glimpses of railway workers. Note the rising smoke at the end: a train approaching from the parallel track? | The fact that this item was advertised in the press with that 'Beautiful Panorama' title suggests that Mitchell and Kenyon, when circumstances demanded, were quite capable of seeing the commercial value of pictorial subjects, as distinct from their more usual crowds of recognisable faces. It's also possible that the filmmakers felt more comfortable with a more visual subject and formal style than usual when they found themselves so far off their familiar northern turf. Ironically, though, the photography here is not particularly outstanding. The operator seems to be s...

video_title: Blue Coat Children Marching to Curzon Hall, Birmingham (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Schools; Parades and marches; Education; Birmingham; Carriage driving (equestrian sport) | locais: Birmingham | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:RmbDdtcDrgVmnynOVa5EZ3sXvzHFl8zI | School procession of boys and girls. | Church school pupils set off for a trip to the famous Birmingham entertainment hall. | Birmingham's Blue Coat School (established 1722) was one of many charity schools founded by the Church of England to educate children from poor families. Garbed in Tudor-period frocks - dyed blue, the traditional colour of almsgiving - the boys would have made a quaint sight as they were marched briskly to what was presumably supposed to be a fun outing.

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Travelogue | assuntos: Landscape; Town planning; Architecture; Buxton; Residential areas | locais: Buxton | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:N4ajdtcDqZq6T_GEyozDlB_f9FZvRVqw | Panoramic view of Buxton. | A striking panorama of the Peak District town and its magnificent Georgian Crescent. | The imposing sight of Buxton's Georgian Crescent and the 18th century Devonshire Dome behind it is as familiar to locals today as it would have been to this film's Edwardian audience. Commissioned from travelling filmmakers Mitchell and Kenyon by exhibitor Mr Tweedale, the film was shown in February 1901 in the Pavilion theatre, part of the then unfinished Buxton Opera House. | For Buxton Skyline, Mitchell and Kenyon made a relatively rare departure from their more typical scenes of bustling crowds. The long, slow panning shot is unusually ambitious for this early period in film's history, and it's not surprising that keeping the camera steady proved a challenge too far given the technology available.

video_title: Cinematographic View of the Royal Albert Bridge (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Battleships; Royal Albert Bridge; Devonport; Plymouth Sound | locais: Royal Albert Bridge | som: Silent | duração: 4 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:9saDdtcDrspxUxQgch0bkKT9q3EzMp8J | Note: The town seen on the mainland is possibly Devonport and the large building the Royal Naval Barracks with a brief glimpse of the Devonport Dockyard beyond. A clock tower is visible and a faint outline of a large church which may be the Barracks chapel. The opening shot may have been filmed in Plymouth Sound. | A view of the many sea-going vessels around Saltash and Plymouth. | Mitchell and Kenyon take their camera out to sea, filming views of the coastline and the mouth of the River Tamar teeming with vessels powered by oar, sail or steam. The Royal Naval College at Plymouth also appears. Anchored off-shore are some Nelson-era battleships, used by cadets for training purposes. Finally, we see the Royal Albert Bridge, built in 1859 by Isambard Kingdom Brunel. | This is one of the southernmost films in the Mitchell and Kenyon collection. The filmmakers travelled widely, visiting Scotland, Wales and Ireland as well as the North of England, but their surviving films suggest that they rarely ventured south of the Midlands.
video_title_display: Cinematographic View of the Royal Albert Bridge (1901)

video_title: Congregation Leaving St Mary's Church, Dumfries (c.1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Dumfries; Worshippers; St Mary's Church (Dumfries) | locais: Dumfries | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:IxZjdtcDr0bG3FoJzB789hq_Bhs6QxrC | Church Exit. | Worshippers in their Sunday best file out of the Church. | An apparently unstoppable flood of worshippers pours down the steps of their church. Top hats and floral hats are in abundance, suggesting a well-heeled congregation. St Mary's Church, built in 1839, survives to this day and belongs to the Church of Scotland. As ever in Mitchell & Kenyon films, it's the children who show the most unembarrassed curiosity in the presence of the camera.
video_title_display: Congregation Leaving St Mary's Church, Dumfries (c.1901)

video_title: Congregation Leaving Wesleyan Chapel in Mansfield (c.1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Methodism; Mansfield; Worshippers; Chapels | locais: Mansfield | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:dzYzdtcDp88LuRhlekkywDRqPI3Mr3Y- | Church Exit. | A view of Mansfield's Bridge Street, as worshippers emerge from chapel. | Thanks to the camera's position on the other side of Bridge Street, this is more of a general street view than a study of a congregation emerging. The chapel entrance isn't shown, making it hard to distinguish between worshippers and passers-by, although the first group would presumably be better dressed. Two boys seem determined to linger in shot throughout, but eventually join their friends. | Mansfield's Wesleyan Chapel was built in 1864, its predecessor having been judged too small to cope with the various local Wesleyan religious revivals. The building, now known as the Bridge Street Methodist Church, remains in use to this day.
video_title_display: Congregation Leaving Wesleyan Chapel in Mansfield (c.1901)

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Preston; Horse-drawn transport; St John's (Preston); Worshippers | locais: Preston | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:dtZzdtcDoXpyUaPA7nsBPpVnB9DhHoJZ | Note: There is no full shot of the Church; only the first few feet above ground are visible. | Preston's great and good gather outside the magnificent St. John's church. | In contrast to many Mitchell and Kenyon films, where the camera is positioned at ground level as close to its subject as possible, the operator has carefully chosen a raised vantage point where his presence would be less obtrusive. Could this be a mark of respect to those attending a religious service? Or does it reflect the filmmakers' fear of upsetting the visibly wealthy congregation?

video_title: Crowd Entering St George's Hall, Bradford (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Bradford; Boys; West Yorkshire; 1900-1909; Audiences; Spectators | locais: Saint George's Hall | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:94bzBtcDqO8HsdLhscvaviIBc_bStt2x | Crowds of boys Note: Features Waller Jeffs who was the manager of all moving pictures shown at this venue, as well as A. D. Thomas, who commissioned this piece of boys going to see themselves on film. | Bradford boys wait eagerly for the picture show at the famous St George's Hall. | Huge crowds throng outside St George's Hall Bradford on a wet February day in 1901, waiting to see a film show of 300 'animated pictures' by the Edison Co. Young boys are much to the fore (this may be the queue for the cheap seats), enthusiastically playing up for the camera. Newspaper reports tell us that this very film of goings on outside the hall, would later play inside as part of the show.

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Street scenes; Horse-drawn transport; Darwen | locais: Darwen | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:42ODdtcDrAJ2g6n-GpWHB8JLUiJ6EE51 | Young lads are out in force on the crowded streets in Edwardian Lancashire. | The first electric tram to Darwen, near Blackburn, ran in 1900. This film, shot the following year, shows a pair of them in the town's bustling centre weaving between a mass of people and other traffic, including a horse-drawn laundry van. The lads in the foreground grow livelier as the film progresses, jumping up and down enthusiastically - probably at the prompting of the filmmakers.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Dockers; Liverpool | locais: Alexandra Dock | som: Silent | duração: 4 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:k1NTdtcDqt0U4j-6hZcbpz0Eoxg6Tf74 | Note: A man, aged mid thirties, wearing a long coat, suit, flat cap, sporting a moustache and carrying an umbrella, is seen throughout the film. He tries to guide the workers to walk to the camera, buys a newspaper, and weaves in and out the traffic. He may be part of the personnel of Mitchell and Kenyon. | Edwardian workers leave the Liverpool docks at the end of another long shift. | At the turn of the 20th century, Britain's shipbuilding yards were the envy of the world. Docks in Liverpool and elsewhere were twice as productive as their American counterparts. The steel columns of the overhead railway, the famous 'dockers umbrella', make this unmistakeably Liverpool. The workers weave through the oncoming traffic towards the camera, a hard day's labour etched in their faces. | Like most of Mitchell & Kenyon's commissioned work, the emphasis here is on capturing as many of the faces of these potential filmgoers as possible as they go about their business. This film, though, is less effectively stage-managed than others. The workers trickle rather than flood past the camera, and at one point two carts, loaded with sacks, sto...

video_title: Employees Leaving Brown's Atlas Works, Sheffield (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Sheffield; Child labour | locais: Sheffield | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:pjNDdtcDptqZzRHu0AbIgm5Mj-adDBzD | Engineering factory gate. | Workers are marshalled past the gate of one of Yorkshire's biggest steel firms. | Staging is common in Mitchell and Kenyon's 'factory gate' films. These workers at John Brown & Co.'s huge Sheffield ironworks are very obviously being chivvied past the camera by a smartly-dressed, bowler-hatted man - possibly Isaac Thomas, brother of regular M&K commissioner AD Thomas. In the concluding shot, an assortment of workers and children wave cheerfully to the camera. | Sheffield-born industrialist Sir John Brown (1816-96) was a major figure in the south Yorkshire iron trade. He founded his company in 1844, and opened the Atlas Works in Brightside in 1856: before the decade was out, the site had grown to 30 acres. Brown made his fortune making rails for the rapidly expanding rail network before turning to armour plate for the Royal Navy. He was knighted for services to British industry in 1867. Mitchell and Kenyon filmed the factory the year before Brown's merged with neighbouring Thomas Firth & Sons and began specialising in stainless steel manufacture. The company still exists as part of...
video_title_display: Employees Leaving Brown's Atlas Works, Sheffield (1901)

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Leeds; Child labour; Fowler's Iron Works | locais: Leeds | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:hqMzdtcDpJZRgAbHeX7H25KoLvpQ4HvD | FACTORY GATE. | A beautifully composed film of Edwardian factory workers as they rush for home. | Nothing remains today of John Fowler & Co.'s engineworks off Leathley Road in Hunslet, Leeds - but in 1901, when this film was made, the company was a leading manufacturer of agricultural and railway steam engines. This strikingly well-composed film of the workforce leaving the factory gives a powerful impression of the scale of human labour required by Edwardian industrial endeavour.

video_title: Employees Leaving Messrs Vickers, Sons & Maxim in Barrow (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Shipbuilding; Munitions; Child labour; Vickers & Maxim; Barrow-in-Furness | locais: Barrow-In-Furness | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:hvMjdtcDrooYVNU3JJpJ3guhIXNb3uCW | Note: Vickers & Maxim were shipbuilders and a munitions company. Vickers (shipbuilders) and Maxim (armaments) merged in 1897. | Edwardian workers file out of a naval shipyard at Barrow-in-Furness. | High walls and imposing wooden gates mark the entrance to a shipbuilding empire as workers leave Vickers, Sons and Maxim's massive Barrow naval construction yard. Older employees appear indifferent to the camera - unlike the young boys, who lark around in the foreground, making faces and even doing cheeky 'V' signs. Amid the laddish tomfoolery, two young girls can also be seen in the crowd.
video_title_display: Employees Leaving Messrs Vickers, Sons & Maxim in Barrow (1901)

video_title: Employees Leaving Tangyes Engineering Works, Smethwick (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Engineering; Birmingham; Smethwick | locais: Birmingham | som: Silent | duração: 3 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:xqMTdtcDopjlgi4VvQ-aAmso-T_Scibq | Crowds of men and boys leaving factory. | Exuberant scenes of engineers and machinists at the famous Cornwall works near Birmingham. | In 1901, Tangyes' was a respected engineering company, specialising in hydraulic systems, having risen to fame after making the lifting-jack that launched Brunel's SS Great Eastern. In sharp close-up, the dust and grease smeared on the clothes and grinning faces of the men are almost tangible. Youthful chaos and cheeky nose-thumbing at the camera suggest a relaxed and happy workplace.
video_title_display: Employees Leaving Tangyes Engineering Works, Smethwick (1901)

video_title: Employees Leaving Vickers, Sons & Maxim Works, Sheffield (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Vickers | locais: Sheffield | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:pkdzZtcDr6W8Wdky8IjVNuEse06TNd-3 | Engineering factory gate. Note: It is a wet day, some men carry opened umbrellas and many wear coats. There are fewer boys than is usual in the `factory gate' films and less acknowledgment of camera. Vickers Ltd was one of the largest manufacturing companies in the world established by Thomas Edward Vickers and specialised in ships, guns, railways and engineering products. | A drizzly street scene outside one of south Yorkshire's major employers. | Unusually, the buildings are the dominant feature in this 'factory gate' film; the workers are relegated to the bottom half of the frame. Vickers, a major name in British engineering, was then one of the largest manufacturers on the globe, specialising in transport materials and munitions. Mitchell and Kenyon's camera also visited the same company's Barrow shipbuilding works.
video_title_display: Employees Leaving Vickers, Sons & Maxim Works, Sheffield (1901)

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Lancaster | locais: Lancaster | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:42djZtcDpA7q_vhPBAKJAhpBq-CyV2Ig | Cotton factory gate. Workers exit Williamson's Factory in Lancaster. Note: Williamson's was a world-renowned manufacturer of linoleum. | Lancastrian workers spill out of a huge textile factory. | Men and boys (and the odd smartly-dressed girl) leave the place of their toil, the monolithic Williamson's Factory pictured in the background. While most depart on foot or bicycle, a few weary souls catch a ride from a passing horse and cart. Established in the 1840s, the successful Williamson family business specialised in the manufacture of coated fabrics, including linoleum and oilcloth.

video_title: Employees Leaving Yates, Haywood & Co. Foundry, Rotherham (1900)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Employees; Rotherham | locais: Rotherham | som: Silent | duração: 3 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:IydjZtcDrqLhp_p7U6sjZxkC1Ggh6Gn9 | Workers leaving the Yates, Haywood & Co building. | Victorian ironworkers emerge from the grand entrance of a long-established South Yorkshire firm. | Unusually, these workers leave through the building's front entrance, perhaps indicating that they had just been paid. The boys behave better than in many 'factory gate' films, possibly due to the presence of a burly police sergeant by the entrance. One man sports a wooden leg, although his real leg remains intact, sticking out behind at right angles - could this be relief for a temporary injury? | The name Yates, Haywood & Co dates back to 1846, but this firm of ironfounders had its origins as early as 1823, when James Yates took over the remains of the old Walkers' Foundry. The firm came under new management in 1911, but the name was retained until the company finally closed down in 1970.
video_title_display: Employees Leaving Yates, Haywood & Co. Foundry, Rotherham (1900)

video_title: Employees of Blundell's Paint Works, Hull (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Advertising; Kingston upon Hull; Posters; Child labour; Paint; Blundell's | locais: Kingston upon Hull | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:BydTZtcDqkxdDpxXNEv-TmYY9mc3DaBI | Tired Edwardian workers finish their shift at a Kingston-Upon-Hull paint factory. | This was one of several films made for screening at Hull's Assembly Rooms in April 1901. Many workers ignore the presence of the camera, but the younger lads are more inquisitive. The view of the factory gate is interrupted by a constant stream of horse-drawn vehicles and the recently introduced electric trams, which pass so close to the camera that you can almost feel the ground shake. | A review in Hull Daily Mail, 30 April 1901, comments that "reference must be made to the local scenes. They form an attractive feature, and although they pass rapidly before the vision, it is an easy matter to distinguish acquaintances".

video_title: Employees of Co-Operative Wholesale Society Printing Works, Longsight, Manchester (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Factories; Women workers; Child labour; Co-operative Wholesale Society; Printing; Longsight | locais: Longsight | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:VmdTZtcDrfXc26XjH92JBqt2znqlNIJK | Factory gate. | Workers co-operate with the camera at a Manchester print factory. | It's hard not to be struck by the number of beaming smiling faces as these workers walk arm in arm towards the camera. They had good reason to be happy: working conditions at this Co-operative owned enterprise, which probably produced print labels and packaging for the Society's vast range of products, would have been far superior to almost any privately run works.
video_title_display: Employees of Co-Operative Wholesale Society Printing Works, Longsight, Manchester (1901)

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Women workers | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:c2cjZtcDrDDIF2HVNWJPh6BzM7-k2ir2 | Note: The pieces of paper are not pay slips. They are advertising leaflets telling the workers when the film show will be. | An unidentified workplace is the scene of a vivid slice of turn-of-the-century life. | 'Pay' was the only word inscribed on this film's original negative, though the pieces of paper being doled out by the young boys aren't cash or, probably, anything similar - most likely they advertise a screening of this very film. We've little else to go on, but there's compensation enough in the unusually close camera, which catches every detail in the faces of the workers and excited children. | The downside of the camera position is that we see almost nothing of the workplace - bar its imposing but not very distinctive gates - so we may never find out exactly what or where it was. But the largely female workforce, and their familiar shawls, might suggest that this was a textile mill, perhaps in the North West.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Royal Navy; Sunderland; Battleships; Sailors; Frigates | locais: Newcastle upon Tyne | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:w4ODNrcDpTmtxrMZgwJvFNtPV2Kl6ivw | Destroyers from the Edwardian Royal Navy filmed from a ship in the Tyne. | This film seems to show a visit of the Royal Navy's Channel Fleet outside the Tyne estuary, on the Sunderland side. The picture has considerable movement despite an apparently calm day, so must have been taken from a moving boat. Several destroyers pass across the camera's field of vision. The local newspaper reports that this group of destroyers belonged to the 'fighting 5th' battle squadron.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Street scenes; Glasgow; Trams; Horse-drawn transport; Hayrides | locais: Glasgow | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:B0bTZtcDoOkG7x_JXSWKqicus43wcmep | Shows horse drawn transport. | A flood of pedestrians and horse-drawn traffic in Edwardian Glasgow. | Jamaica Street was and still is one of Glasgow's busiest thoroughfares, and that's probably why Mitchell and Kenyon took their camera there in April 1901. Showman AD Thomas, who distributed this film, claimed that some 8,000 people saw it screened at the city's Grand Circus. Many of those will have been among the flood of workers in the concluding shot, apparently taken in another street. | The film illustrates a world in flux: many of the horse-drawn buses and carriages seen here would be replaced by electric trams a year later, in 1902. The traffic is almost nose-to-tail, but seems very orderly all the same.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Liverpool; Motor cars; Hotels; Car dealers | locais: Liverpool | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:l3d2pvcDq_oGyMX2HUyZRwj3TlakruPY | M&K 406: Liverpool Street Scenes. Stationary shot of street activity outside a department store. | Traffic and pedestrians - including an early motor car - in Edwardian Liverpool. | Seen from the city's 'Holy Corner', Liverpool is a hive of people and traffic: in the early 1900s much trade was conducted on the streets. Liverpool had become a wealthy city, and the shop fronts are filled with items for sale. Unusually, we see a motor car, a potent symbol of prosperity. One of the passengers may be Sagar Mitchell - half of filmmaking duo Mitchell and Kenyon. | Two rolls of film were used to make up this one film and it would have proved of great interest to local people, many of whom will have paid between sixpence and a hefty two shillings in the hope of catching a glimpse of themselves on screen. The film would also have been seen as great advertising for the vehicle and its seller, and for all the shops featured.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Documentary | assuntos: Statues; Opening ceremonies; Manchester; Roberts, Lord Frederick (1832-1914); Boer War (1899-1902); Camera operators | locais: Manchester | som: Silent | duração: 3 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:NhN215cTrul5-ycH1e1q1ieOQ-rK1Ay- | M&K 420: Visit of Earl Frederick Roberts to Manchester. | The Boer War hero addresses a capacity crowd in Manchester's Piccadilly Gardens and unveils a statue to the late Queen Victoria. | The capacity crowds demonstrate the celebrity enjoyed by the Boer War leaders - this is one of several Mitchell and Kenyon films featuring generals recently back from South Africa. Lord Roberts addresses the crowds as he unveils Edward Onslow Ford's statue of Victoria. There's an intriguing gentleman in a Fez, plus a rare glimpse of film pioneer Cecil Hepworth vying for position with his camera. | Lord Roberts returned from South Africa around the end of 1900, after a series of decisive victories had turned the course of the war in Britain's favour.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: School Children | locais: Dumfries | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:R1czFtcDqtk2zjv4Ta5lfe07xmuNbWNz | Dumfries schoolchildren take their turn in the spotlight. | One of three films shot for Mitchell and Kenyon - possibly all on the same visit - in the market town of Dumfries, this charming item captures children, the youngest around five, from a local school. Despite miserable weather, the children's smiles are wide, and the cameraman crams as many as possible into the frame, in the hope of encouraging local parents to see their offspring on screen later. | The strict formality of the Edwardian education system is reflected in the separate lines of well-behaved young boys and girls. At one point the film's showman sponsor is seen throwing something - probably sweets or pennies - into the throng to provoke a good-natured scramble. This kind of technique was often employed by the filmmakers to generate excitement and entertainment for the audience. It certainly brings some energy to what might otherwise have been a slightly stiff scene.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Spiritualism; Manchester; Parades and marches | locais: Manchester | som: Silent | duração: 3 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:BrZThwcDqz0YQmsQ0rm1ZLLwpp6F1SLd | Procession of Manchester Spiritualists. | Exponents of Britain's newest religion don their Sunday best for a parade through Edwardian Manchester. | Spiritualism emerged in the mid-19th century and promoted the belief that the dead can communicate with the living. By 1901 the controversial new religion boasted millions of followers in the US and Britain, and not one but three reels of film were lavished on this lively procession. The smart dresses, hats and suits attest to the Spiritualist Church's largely middle-class congregation. | The film was made by showman Arthur Duncan Thomas, who passed himself off as the American inventor Thomas Edison. "Edison Will Reproduce this Procession in Animated Photograph Twice Daily, St. James' Hall Manchester", reads a promotional placard seen in the film. Contemporary viewers may be surprised to see so many younger participants - among the colourful banners, including one held aloft by members of the 'Royal Spiritual Lyceum', one slogan reads "Open the door for the children". Despite a resurgence of popularity in the 1920s, the movement went into sharp decline, but...

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Street scenes; British Army; Manchester; Shops; Trams; Horse-drawn transport; Carts; 1900-1909; Boer War (1899-1902) | locais: Manchester | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:I0ZThwcDrsb5-YohMpLnHkL7LfhstJT7 | Scenes of Market Street in Manchester. | A rare glimpse of early Edwardian Manchester when the horse-drawn tram still reigned supreme. | These scenes of Market Street, still Manchester's busiest shopping area, encapsulate the bustling energy of the city centre in the Edwardian era. 1901 saw the inauguration of the city's first electric tram route, which would soon replace the horse-drawn method depicted here. Showman A.D. Thomas is seen distributing tickets for the forthcoming travelling film show to pedestrians. | The film was included in a programme of films titled 'Life in Manchester' shown daily at St James's Hall throughout the summer of 1901. It offers rich insights into various aspects of urban life, including the growing confidence of women, who are shown wandering freely around the city centre. The inclusion of the long-take of the Boer soldier and the blond-haired boy (probably the soldier's son) reflects contemporary public interest in the ongoing Anglo-Boer war, which was widely covered in contemporary media and in popul...

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Pageants; Manchester; Bicycles; Impersonations; Roberts, Lord Frederick (1832-1914) | locais: Manchester | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:UxajhwcDoed9ml9_9kVCn0q22L7FF4iH | Cycle parade of comic characters, probably in Manchester. | A small boy impersonates military hero Lord Roberts, much to the delight of the crowds. | One of the star attractions of this charity parade in aid of local institutions is a small boy perched on a horse, magnificently attired as the 19th century military hero, Lord Roberts. After little Lord 'Bobs' has been caught on film, a crowd of onlookers flood into shot cheering and waving, encouraged by the filmmakers to express their enthusiasm for the benefit of the camera.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Explosions; Black people; Miners; Child labour; Pendlebury Colliery; Pendlebury | locais: Pendlebury | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:BocjFtcDqn6rrbAuMyIwn6iTcjidlNbP | Factory exit from pit-head at Pendlebury Colliery. Shot for Pendlebury Wakes in Manchester in August 1901. Note: A black miner is among the crowd. Sedgwick himself, Bioscope Fairground Showmen, who commissioned the piece can be seen directing the movements of workers. Information provided by Dr. Toulmin, National Fairground Archive, University of Sheffield. | A black miner makes an intriguing addition to an already captivating film of coal workers up from their pit. | Showman Albert Sedgwick, conspicuously raffish in homburg and moustache, bestrides and controls this film. Sedgwick has 'planted' the poster board advertising the show in which it's to be screened - as well as, probably, the Afro-Caribbean man whose presence in Edwardian Lancashire so surprises us. And he is choreographing, right before our eyes, the procession of colliers for the camera. | This film was included in Tate Britain's 2013 retrospective of L.S. Lowry's art. Lowry had himself lived in Pendlebury - although he didn't move there until 1912, 11 years after this film was shot. It's not impossible...

video_title: Morecambe Church Lads Parade at Drill (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Keep fit; Morecambe; Boys' Brigade | locais: Morecambe | som: Silent | duração: 3 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:1rN2xjcjpoZRQLtPLhDK49K61Ypk2azN | Larking about during Church Lads' Brigade drill and parade in Morecambe. | Moving pictures were already a popular entertainment in Morecambe by 1901, and this film was apparently a hit when shown alongside other local films, including Mitchell and Kenyon's Panoramic View of the Morecambe Sea Front, at the Lancashire resort's Winter Gardens. "Needless to say these pictures are followed with intense interest," said a Morecambe Visitor reporter who was in the audience. | Like several other films in the Mitchell & Kenyon collection, this film was shot not by Mitchell & Kenyon themselves but by Thomas-Edison Pictures, the company run by flamboyant showman and regular M&K collaborator AD Thomas.The Church Lads' Brigade was formed in 1891, inspired by the Boys' Brigade (formed eight years earlier). Similar youth organisations, typically with a quasi-military ethos, were on the increase during the Edwardian period, which also saw the rise of the Girls' Brigade and the Scouts movements.
video_title_display: Morecambe Church Lads Parade at Drill (1901)

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Morecambe; Worshippers; Holy Trinity with Saint Lawrence | locais: Morecambe | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:9ubjFtcDrAJYXw1DLnxfz4TRCmm5bgYx | Note: There is no full shot of Holy Trinity with Saint Lawrence. | Worshippers stroll out of church after the Sunday service. | Morecambe was a relatively short journey from Mitchell and Kenyon's Blackburn base, and the filmmakers seem to have made at least two visits to the Lancashire coast resort between 1901 and 1902. The well-spruced churchgoers in this film are less numerous than the abundant crowds typical in Mitchell and Kenyon's films, in which they made a point of capturing as many people on camera as possible.

video_title: Operatives Leaving Messrs Pilkington Bros. Works, St Helens (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Factories; Women workers; St. Helens; Glass; Child labour; Pilkington Glass Works | locais: St Helens | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:VrZTVrcDrcoZoCDBXAhewtVqs9N8kLC0 | Factory gate. | Shift change at Edwardian Britain's most important glassmakers, at the heart of the Merseyside town. | Some factories are so much the centre of their town that they begin to define it. 'Pilks' wasn't yet the industrial giant it would become, but it was big enough that Mitchell and Kenyon's cameraman needed several different positions to capture just some of its workers outside the factory gates. The smaller children waiting for their families may before long have ended up working there themselves.

video_title: Operatives of Acme Spinning Company, Pendlebury (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Women workers; Spinning; Textile industry; Child labour; Pendlebury; Pendlebury Spinning Co. | locais: Pendlebury | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:NlbTFtcDr2LbU09NoucXXW8TJ21dUhyX | Filmmaker James Kenyon moves amid the hoards of workers leaving Acme Spinning Works in Pendlebury. | A glimpse of early filmmaking showmanship as James Kenyon marshals Lancashire textile workers. | Filmmaker James Kenyon - half of the Mitchell & Kenyon duo - can be seen opening the gates at the start of this film and moving through the mass of workers. The film offers rare insight into the pair's showman tactics, designed to liven up the scene. Kenyon briefly hides behind one woman, to much hilarity. Painter LS Lowry lived in Pendlebury, and drew inspiration from textile mills like this one.

video_title: Panoramic View of the Morecambe Sea Front (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Morecambe; Piers; Promenades | locais: Morecambe | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:syMjhwcDqv7M9EcjFbQfRUKDpqTuSxvy | M&K 254: Note: The West End Pier pavilion building was opened in 1896 and was destroyed by fire in 1915. The film is particularly good for illustrating the range of clothes worn by the men, women and children. | Crystal-clear views of town, sands, sea, tourists and locals at the Lancashire resort. | With their summer afternoon clarity, Mitchell and Kenyon's images of Morecambe remain gorgeously fresh. The respectable pleasures of the white-collar resort are communicated by static shots (men, women and children on the promenade) and others filmed from a horse-drawn tram: onlookers are encouraged to wave, while excited boys race the carriage. The seaside shimmers in the background. | The film ends on a wave across the street and a view of the poster advertising the winter gardens show at which this film, commissioned and exhibited by the flamboyant showman AD Thomas, was first screened. It subsequently travelled at least as far as Yorkshire (there's evidence of it being screened in Bradford).
video_title_display: Panoramic View of the Morecambe Sea Front (1901)

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: School Children; Widnes | locais: Widnes | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:ZzazFtcDp7y2RN0LUtkAxtUGGOhqhDtr | Scenes of schoolchildren filing past the camera. | A multitude of Lancashire schoolchildren is ushered past the camera. | A crowd of young boys scampers past, mildly shepherded by female teachers. Some more tightly marshalled older groups follow behind, while pairs of young girls, hand in hand, close the parade. The children are in such profusion that we seem to have seen the entire youth of the then Lancashire (now Cheshire) town.

video_title: Parade on West End Pier Morecambe (1901)

Visible on the right hand side is a queue of people who are being directed by the exhibitors to turn around ready to make their screen debut.

video_title: Police Annual Inspection at Birchfields Park (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Police; Manchester; Parades and marches; Legislation | locais: Manchester | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:d3eDdwcDrog8ay-1huY50VXyucMrKeNu | M&K 439: Passing out parade of policemen in Manchester with mounted police. | Edwardian Manchester bobbies are put through their paces. | The filmmakers position their cameras to great effect in these scenes of formation marching. The massed ranks of Manchester's police officers and their horses and bicycles rhythmically criss-cross the frame. No doubt to the embarrassment of the officers concerned, the camera catches the skittish disarray of horses and the fall of a rider and mount in front of the VIP gathering.
video_title_display: Police Annual Inspection at Birchfields Park (1901)

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: Preston; Local customs; Eggs; Easter; Egg rolling | locais: Preston | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:owazFtcDqtxyYdTTr7Jlv3vyC4G7rGL3 | Eccelesiastal and secular celebration of Easter. | Easter Monday festivities in Avenham Park, Preston. | Mitchell and Kenyon filmed several local traditions, including this Easter custom in Preston's Avenham Park, which dates centuries and is still observed today. One of the showmen who commissioned the film doffs his top hat to the camera before gamely joining in. It's a communal occasion and a time to show off - one mother displays a baby nearly smothered in its swaddling clothes. | The movement for public parks peaked in the late Victorian and Edwardian years, fuelled by a belief that the mingling of the classes would be good for social cohesion, and by the need to provide the working classes with outdoor spaces away from the sinful pleasures of the tavern or music hall. Eleven parks can be seen among the films in the Mitchell and Kenyon collection, each of them an arena for physical exercise and civic pride.

Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: State visits; Birkenhead; Monarchy; Thailand; Sea ferries | locais: Liverpool | som: Silent | duração: 3 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:x3ZzFtcDqAXGSy1VoDf8JN0cEEeNpS2_ | The future King of Siam takes a trip across the Mersey. | Crown Prince Vajiravudh of Siam (now Thailand) was educated in Britain, first at Sandhurst from 1892 and then at Christ Church College, Oxford. He may still have been at Oxford when this film was taken. The Prince was on a visit to Port Sunlight with the radio pioneer Guglielmo Marconi, and they would have taken this route across the Mersey to the nearby New Ferry on the Wirral Peninsular. | On the same trip the Prince and Marconi also visited the SS Lucania to inspect the ship's new wireless telegraphy equipment. The Prince later served briefly with the British Army before returning to Siam in 1903. He was crowned King of Siam in 1910.

video_title: Proclamation of King Edward VII, Market Place, Accrington (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Lancashire; Police officers; Ceremonies; King Edward VII (1841-1910); Mayors; Monarchy; Spectators; Accrington; Civic buildings; Proclamations | locais: Accrington | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:xvMWNtcDqOEfC0Z7aa6IhWYrqBBJ6g3O | Note: The proclamation may be the formal announcement of the accession to the throne of King Edward VII. If this is the case then the film can be dated January 24th 1901. The conductor may be conducting `God Save the King'. | The Edwardian era kicks off with the proclamation of the new King. | A momentous occasion it certainly was, but a formal declaration isn't perhaps the most visually appealing subject for a silent film. All the same, the townspeople of Accrington would have been out in force, meaning plenty of potential customers for Mitchell and Kenyon and this film's commissioner, Ralph Pringle. One man on the platform appears to conduct the crowd in a song (the national anthem?).
video_title_display: Proclamation of King Edward VII, Market Place, Accrington (1901)

video_title: Ralph Pringle Interviewing Private Ward V.C. Hero in Leeds (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Yorkshire; Soldiers; Victoria Cross; Leeds; Military honours; Interviews; Boer War (1899-1902); Courts martial | locais: Leeds | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:JmdTBtcDoZOzYldpoTiypufizEh-BBMn | An `interview' with Private Charles Ward (Yorkshire Light Infantry) winner of the Victoria Cross in the Boer War 1901. | An ordinary hero of the Boer War in a curious silent 'interview'. | Private Charles Ward was a Leeds infantryman who returned from the Boer War a local hero, bearer of the last Victoria Cross awarded by the Queen herself. This intriguing item shows him interviewed - silent - by showman and regular Mitchell and Kenyon collaborator Ralph Pringle, who clearly saw Ward's newfound celebrity as an opportunity to attract a large paying audience of proud locals. | The screening of this film was apparently accompanied by the local regiment the Leeds Rifles. It's not clear, though, whether a commentator would have spoken some of Ward's words (though Pringle is seen taking some notes during the interview). Private Ward's Second Battalian Yorkshire Light Infantry was overwhelmed by Boer forces at Lindley, South Africa, in June 1900. With his officers all dead or wounded, Ward crossed enemy lines to deliver a message calling for reinforc...
video_title_display: Ralph Pringle Interviewing Private Ward V.C. Hero in Leeds (1901)

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: Rowing; Preston; River Ribble | locais: Preston | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:kxdTBtcDpo5miOZd3SZOEu1lKBD7Ih4N | An afternoon's boating in the River Ribble at Edwardian Preston. | Crowds of men, women and children, in their Sunday best, mess about in boats on the River Ribble, with the London and North Western Railway bridge visible in the mid-distance. Many of those not rowing wave for the camera. The embankment is as busy as the water, so presumably this is a Sunday or a holiday. Either way, these Edwardians were enjoying a boom in leisure time.

video_title: Ride on a Tram Car through Belfast (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Street scenes; Shops; Bicycles; Trams; Belfast; Advertising; Buses; Horse-drawn transport; Pedestrians; Cycles | locais: Belfast | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:kycjdwcDoVuutZQDBsp7_MwHH-BLOZ2F | M&K 724: Shots from top deck of (unseen) tram travelling along Belfast centre shopping street: sunny weather. | Downtown Belfast is easily recognisable in these scenes from a tramcar ride in 1901. | These strikingly sharp scenes from Belfast present familiar thoroughfares including Bedford Street and Donegall Place. Fashionable residents peruse upmarket shops and we also glimpse passing horse-drawn transport to the Ormeau and Malone Roads. The film appeared as part of a show at the Ulster Hall in May 1901 and billboards advertising the event appear in shots of Royal Avenue.
video_title_display: Ride on a Tram Car through Belfast (1901)

video_title: Royal Proclamation of Death of Queen Victoria, Blackburn (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Mayors; Advertising; Blackburn; Posters; Proclamations; Queen Victoria of Great Britain (1819-1901) | locais: Blackburn | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:ZtdDBtcDp680uvasPXQ0vjNv2Dh0rV8e | Note: This may be a proclamation announcing the accession of King Edward VII which would date the film January 1901. | Crowds gather outside theTown Hall to hear the town crier's royal announcement. | Despite its title, this film appears to show the proclamation of King Edward VII, successor to Queen Victoria - the cheers suggesting the celebration of the new King's accession to the throne. The two announcements of the death of Queen and the accession of the new King would have occurred almost simultaneously, in an unofficial variant on "The king is dead, long live the king."

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Street scenes; Factory workers; Women workers; Crowds; Child labour; Carlisle | locais: Carlisle | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:13cjBtcDqthldEid9DsozIjs7vEPwK0r | Crowds of people leaving work through factory doors in Carlisle | A well dressed crowd emerges from an unknown building and engages with the camera. | It's not possible to identify the building from which this crowd emerges but it's unlikely to be a factory, given their standard of dress. A couple of well-dressed women make a point of filing past the camera to give a cheerful wave. The film was probably commissioned by C. Algie, the Carlisle-based proprietor of Algie's Modern Circus, who worked with related Mitchell & Kenyon exhibitors.

video_title: St George's Day Procession in Liverpool (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Liverpool; St. George's Day | locais: Liverpool | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:9iZTdwcDqHkkI-ZCyxG6viZ7NDh6otvA | St George's Day celebrations in Liverpool. | Remarkable scenes of St George's Day celebrations in early 20th century Merseyside. | Liverpool was a flourishing, prosperous city in 1901 and this display of English patriotism and civic pride was one of several events in the city captured on film that year. We see the throng of onlookers around Nelson's monument and Castle Street as the procession wends its way to St. George's Hall. In one puzzling scene, a Town Hall official points angrily at the cameraman.
video_title_display: St George's Day Procession in Liverpool (1901)

video_title: The Return of the Royal Warwickshire Regiment (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Warwickshire; Parades and marches; Spectators; Armed forces; Volunteer units; Mounted police; Boer War (1899-1902); Military ceremonies | locais: Birmingham | som: Silent | duração: 3 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:c1anJvcDqcmsgkkBu2N66KZnBwhHcQva | Civic procession in Birmingham, March 1901 Boer War related. | Soldiers returning from the Boer War get a warm welcome home from crowds in Edwardian Birmingham. | Cameras are placed at various viewpoints, some raised and others at street level, to cover soldiers from the Royal Warwickshire Regiment and their police escort marching into Birmingham's Victoria Square in May 1901. The massed crowds are largely corralled behind barriers, and some later complained about the poor views. No such problems, though, faced Mitchell and Kenyon's cameramen.

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Manchester Ship Canal; Royal Navy; Motor torpedo boats | locais: Manchester | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:xoZnJvcDoGREJ4HFAxuNfilTmmDl_rlp | The might of the British Navy struts its stuff down the Manchester Ship Canal. | Eight high speed torpedo boats and their crew visit Edwardian Manchester. The two Barton bridges over the Manchester Ship Canal open for the flotilla to pass through - these are swing bridges, one for a road, the other the only swing aqueduct in Britain carrying a canal. The torpedo boats exude raw power as they pass, observed by two camera crews, one on either side of the canal.

video_title: Trip to Sunny Vale Gardens at Hipperholme (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Donkeys; Fairground rides; Hipperholme; Sunny Vale Gardens; Boating (recreational) | locais: Hipperholme | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:N0NDdwcDrTlFrdpsOvj3QQ6grfUXy2uK | M&K 588: Groups of people assembled in front of a gate and then walk towards camera. Men let people through the gate, taking money and issuing tickets | Summertime laughs and larks at a West Yorkshire pleasure gardens. | These scenes were filmed at West Yorkshire's Sunny Vale pleasure gardens for their owner, Joseph Bunce. On this sunny summer's day the gardens are full of people in Sunday best enjoying swings and rides, music and dancing. The film adds staged comic scenes (perhaps Bunce and his sons), including a young man in women's clothing falling during a donkey race, showing an expanse of frilly knickers.

video_title: Turn Out of the Leeds Fire Brigade (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Firefighting; Advertising; Fire brigades; Horse-drawn transport; Leeds; Human signs; Fire engines; West Yorkshire; 1900-1909; Mediaeval styles and periods; Fire stations | locais: Leeds | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:1hbjBtcDpymMPdhyk6DlAcw-8wRsaqk7 | Scenes in Leeds city centre as Edwardian firemen roll through on drill. | For all its silence, this film is weirdly evocative of the sounds of the Edwardian street: the thundering of iron-shod hooves on cobbled streets, the clanging of fire engines' bells, the shouts and laughs of the crowd. The shot of firemen assembling for a photograph is a real highlight: their merry tomfoolery is a world apart from the austere still portraits we're used to seeing.

video_title: Turn Out of the Newcastle Fire Brigade (1901)

The making of the film was an event in itself, and helps explain the large audience and the presence of so many local dignitaries, clearly visible in both of the film's two shots.

video_title: Turn Out of the Preston Fire Brigade (c.1901)

The man in the foreground with bowler hat and pipe is probably the film's commissioner, George Green, who would have been encouraging the crowds to attend the screening in a local venue not long after this footage was shot.

video_title: Tynemouth Swimming Gala in the Haven, North Shields (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Diving (sports); Bathing; Entertainment and recreation; North Shields; Swimming competitions | locais: Tynemouth | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:ZzbDBtcDpl6R6upx-0LC3NTyA0N4rNvV | Edwardian crowds enjoy a fancy-dress swimming race. | These suited, top-hatted gentlemen make for an odd spectacle as they bob up and down with opened umbrellas in the waters of the Tyne at North Shields. The film shows the Tynemouth Amateur Swimming Club Annual Gala, with its '60 yards Comic Obstacle Race', held on 2nd September 1901. It's a delightful record of the humour and community spirit of Edwardians at play. | Contemporary local newspaper reports show that this bit of fun was shot by the exhibitor and regular Mitchell and Kenyon collaborator Mr Tweedale, and was shown as part of a two-hour programme at the Tynemouth Palace with other more serious films, including the women workers of the North Sea Fisheries, also part of the M&K collection.

video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film; Sport | assuntos: Rugby matches; Warrington | locais: Warrington | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:VyazBtcDr4WMAt6MDkjq5CrX3jiyjym- | Scenes at a rugby match - one team wears hooped shirts with dark shorts, the other striped shirts with light shorts. | Match action and scenes of an Edwardian rugby crowd at Warrington's Wilderspool stadium. | Lancashire's St Helens, in hooped shirts, visited Cheshire rivals Warrington for this early Edwardian game. As in other Mitchell and Kenyon rugby films, we can see here an early phase of the evolution of Northern Union rules: note the quickly-formed scrum after each tackle. Shots along the touchline show officials and spectators transfixed by the play, while others are distracted by the camera. | The Northern Rugby Football Union, better known as Northern Union, was formed in 1895, when prominent Yorkshire and Lancashire clubs resigned from the Rugby Football Union in a dispute over compensation for players taking time off work. All the major differences between the two codes would be established by 1907.

Gossage and Sons. Located on the west Bank of the St Helen's Canal in Widnes, the company was responsible for around half of all of the UK's international soap trade at this time. Unlike most 'factory gates' films, which show only building exteriors, this one gives us a glimpse of the glass-roofed interior.

video_title: Workers Leaving Guest and Chrimes Brassworks, Rotherham (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Employees; Rotherham; Industries, trades and crafts; Guest & Chrimes | locais: Rotherham | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:E1ZTBtcDrzegETSvwgEPQkdADtNNA814 | Men and boys leaving the Guest and Chrimes Brassworks, Rotherham | Employees of a South Yorkshire brassworks walk or cycle past the camera. | Probably shot in spring or autumn (it's sunny, but the clothes suggest it wasn't warm), this film shows the entirely male workforce leaving this Rotherham foundry. Most are on foot, although two have bicycles. They seem more aware of the camera than in similar films, and the younger lads (and some adults) spontaneously form a mini-parade at the very end, waving their caps in the air. | Peter and Edward Chrimes first established a brass foundry in Rotherham's Market Place in 1843, initially specialising in high-pressure taps but quickly moving into fire hydrants, sluice valves and similar items. John Guest joined the firm in 1847, and the firm relocated to its present site in 1857. As of 2013, the building remains standing, although it has been in disuse since the foundry's closure in 1999.
video_title_display: Workers Leaving Guest and Chrimes Brassworks, Rotherham (1901)

video_title: Workers at Berry's Blacking Works, Manchester (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Engineering; Manchester; Trams; Child labour; Berry's; Polish | locais: Manchester | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:V5ajBtcDrT8McDKgnqBTiInyu-s36bfL | Scenes at Berry's Blacking factory in Manchester. | Operatives heading out of Berry's Blacking Works. | Hoards of male workers exit a blacking works in Manchester's Rochdale Road area. Like most Mitchell and Kenyon 'factory gate' films, this was shot from a single fixed viewpoint. The camera captures every nuance of expression on the workers faces, from curiosity to amusement to indifference. Berry's Diamond Oil Blacking, loaded on the passing carts, was the leading shoe polish brand at the time.
video_title_display: Workers at Berry's Blacking Works, Manchester (1901)

video_title: Workers at Carr's Biscuit Works, Carlisle (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Carlisle; Carr's Biscuits | locais: Carlisle | som: Silent | duração: 1 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:VpajBtcDoE5FyvJEvP9lKV07gTtQ1OaB | Factory gate. | Royal Warrant biscuit-makers released after their shift. | Filmed on a gloomy November day, the young factory lads from Carr's seem unusually determined to draw attention to themselves by grinning and waving cheerily at the camera. A local newspaper advert promoting the film as "a picture never before seen in Carlisle" reveals that it was shown a few days after shooting at Algie's Circus, a static circus venue on Carlisle's Botchergate.
video_title_display: Workers at Carr's Biscuit Works, Carlisle (1901)

video_title: Workers at Ross & Hardy Paper Works (1901)
video_description: Registro público materializado no BFI Replay. | disponibilidade: Internet-UK | gêneros: Actuality film | assuntos: Factory workers; Child labour; OSS and Hardy Paper Works | som: Silent | duração: 2 min | Brightcove: 6057949427001/ref:NwaTBtcDpwone2_smDLjemIgM2kuE7Ka | Where was this Edwardian paper works? | By 1901 wood pulp had begun to replace more costly cotton fibre in paper-making, leading to a burgeoning market in cheaply-produced newspapers and other print materials. We don't know where this long-gone works was, but its handsome stuccoed frontage and healthy appearance of its employees - some of them proudly holding beautifully-clothed infants up for the cameras - suggest a thriving business.
